content:
China's AI Models vs My Invoice: A Freelancer's Real-World Test

Last month, a client almost fired me over API costs.

Not because I was overcharging. Because I'd been quietly burning their OpenAI budget on tasks that didn't need GPT-4o. I was using a $10/M output model to clean up CSV files and rewrite product descriptions. That's like hiring a lawyer to notarize a form. By the time I caught it, I'd burned through maybe $600 of their budget on stuff a $0.25/M model could have handled identically.

That was the night I went down a rabbit hole. I spent two full weekends routing real client work through DeepSeek, Qwen, Kimi, and GLM via Global API's unified endpoint. Every request logged. Every dollar tracked. Every quality check graded against what the client actually saw in the deliverable.

This is what I learned, written for anyone else who bills by the hour and 精打细算 (carefully counts pennies).

How I Tested These Models

Quick methodology so you know I'm not making this up. I picked ten recurring tasks from my actual client backlog:

• Rewriting product descriptions for an e-commerce store

• Generating Python functions for a SaaS dashboard

• Translating marketing copy from English to Simplified Chinese

• Summarizing long legal PDFs

• Doing multi-step reasoning over messy customer support logs

• Writing SQL queries against weird schemas

• Generating image alt text (vision tasks)

• Drafting cold outreach emails

• Brainstorming taglines for a brand

• Bulk reformatting JSON responses

Each model got every task. I noted output quality, tokens-per-second felt speed, and the actual line-item cost on my Global API dashboard. No vibes-based reviewing — just billable work and the receipts.

DeepSeek V4 Flash Became My Default

The first time I swapped DeepSeek V4 Flash into a client workflow, I refreshed the billing page three times because I thought the dashboard was broken.

V4 Flash runs $0.25 per million output tokens. I ran a 14,000-token content rewrite job and it cost me roughly $0.0035. On GPT-4o, the same job would have been $0.14. That's a 40x difference on identical output quality for what the client sees.

For my freelance reality, that math is everything. When a client sends me 50 product descriptions to rewrite, I'm not going to charge them 50x more just because I'm paying for a premium model. I was eating the difference. Now I don't have to.

Here's the thing — V4 Flash isn't some bargain-bin compromise model. It clocks around 60 tokens per second, which means I'm not sitting there watching a spinner while the client waits on Slack. It scores top-tier on HumanEval and MBPP for code. English output is on par with the best Western models I've used. It just happens to cost peanuts.

A few caveats from my testing:

• No native vision. If a client sends me a screenshot and says "build this," I have to route that elsewhere.

• Chinese-language output is solid but not best-in-class. If the brief says "appeal to Chinese consumers specifically," I'd still pick GLM.

• Model variety is narrower than Qwen's lineup, but that's never actually mattered for my workload.

The other models in the DeepSeek family have their place too. V3.2 at $0.38/M is what I reach for when I want the latest architecture on a tight budget. V4 Pro at $0.78/M is my "this needs to be production-quality for a paying client who notices everything" option. R1 at $2.50/M is overkill for 90% of my work but pulls its weight on math-heavy debugging. Coder at $0.25/M is essentially V4 Flash tuned for code — I use it interchangeably with the base V4 Flash.

Qwen Is the Duct Tape of My Stack

If DeepSeek is my daily driver, Qwen is the Swiss Army knife I keep in my back pocket.

What sold me is the range. The Qwen lineup spans from Qwen3-8B at $0.01/M all the way up to Qwen3.5-397B at $2.34/M, with a flagship topping out at $3.20/M. That spread means there's a Qwen model for literally anything a client throws at me, and I can bill the right one.

Practical examples from last month:

A startup wanted product image alt text generated for 800 SKUs. Routed through Qwen3-VL-32B at $0.52/M. The model understood the images, wrote SEO-friendly descriptions, and the total bill was under $2. Another client needed audio transcription plus summarization from a podcast. Qwen3-Omni-30B at $0.52/M handled both ends in a single call. A finance client ran multi-step reasoning on quarterly reports — Qwen3.5-397B at $2.34/M was my weapon of choice there.

For pure general-purpose coding and content, Qwen3-32B at $0.28/M is a sweet spot. It's the model I reach for when DeepSeek V4 Flash feels slightly underpowered for a complex task but I still can't justify the GPT-4o bill.

Honest gripes: the naming convention is a mess. Qwen3-something, Qwen3.5, Qwen3.6, with VL, Omni, and Coder variants each spinning off their own sub-line. I've lost twenty minutes of billable time just figuring out which Qwen model handles vision versus which handles audio. Also, mid-range English output is good but doesn't quite hit the DeepSeek ceiling. And yes, Qwen3.6-35B at $1/M feels steep for what you get — I'd rather drop down to Qwen3-32B for almost everything.

Kimi K2.5 Is Expensive — But Clients Don't Argue When It Works

Here's the math problem with Kimi. K2.5 runs $3.00/M output tokens. The other Kimi models in their lineup sit around $3.00–$3.50/M.

That's roughly 12x what I pay for V4 Flash. For a freelancer running on hourly rates, that's the kind of multiple that makes me sweat.

So why do I still use it?

Because some clients pay premium, and some tasks demand premium. A law firm client hired me to build a document analysis pipeline. They were going to charge their own clients $15,000/month for access. My API budget was rounding error. Kimi K2.5 crushed the reasoning benchmarks we needed — multi-hop inference over messy contracts, contradiction detection between paragraphs, the kind of work where a hallucination costs real money.

On that project, the Kimi spend was like $180 across the engagement. The client revenue from the deliverable was in the tens of thousands. ROI was absurd.

The other Kimi models are positioned similarly premium. No "budget tier" exists. If you're billing Kimi work to a client, you can justify the spend. If you're absorbing Kimi costs as overhead, look elsewhere.

Speed is also a tradeoff. K2.5 is the slowest of the four families in my testing. For batch processing overnight, fine. For a synchronous Slack conversation with a client waiting for an answer, I'd reroute.

GLM Is What I Open When the Brief Mentions "Chinese Audience"

GLM has been my secret weapon for Chinese-language work, full stop.

The pricing reflects the spectrum — GLM-4-9B at $0.01/M for tiny tasks, GLM-5 at $1.92/M for top-tier. Most of my Chinese work routes through GLM-5 because the quality gap on Chinese-specific nuance is real. Cultural idioms, formal/informal register, localized business terminology — GLM handles it better than the other three families in side-by-side comparisons.

One client runs a Mandarin-language customer support operation. The previous vendor was routing everything through an English-primary model with a translation layer in front, which produced the kind of awkward Chinglish that makes native speakers cringe. I swapped to direct GLM-5 calls. The client's NPS scores went up. I billed more hours because the work was higher quality.

GLM also ships a vision model, GLM-4.6V, which I use when a client sends me Chinese-language screenshots or scanned documents. Qwen's VL models handle this too, but GLM's Chinese OCR is noticeably tighter in my testing.

The honest weakness: English-language output from GLM is fine but never my first pick. If the deliverable is going to an English-speaking audience, I'm routing through DeepSeek or Qwen.

The Actual Numbers From Last Month

Here's what my Global API invoice looked like for a typical 30-day period, with about 12 active client engagements.

DeepSeek (mostly V4 Flash, some V4 Pro): ~$14

Qwen (mostly Qwen3-32B and Qwen3-VL-32B): ~$22

Kimi (K2.5 for one specific project): ~$43

GLM (mostly GLM-5 for Chinese work): ~$31

Total: $110 for what would have been $700+ on the old OpenAI-only stack.

That $590 monthly difference is roughly 4 billable hours at my rate. It's the difference between "profitable side hustle" and "constantly stressed about burn."

What I'd Recommend If You're Starting Today

Save yourself my two weekends of testing. Here's the stack I'd deploy blind:

Daily driver, 70% of work: DeepSeek V4 Flash at $0.25/M. Best price-to-performance ratio in this whole lineup, possibly in the entire API market.

Vision, audio, specialized tasks: Qwen3-VL-32B or Qwen3-Omni-30B at $0.52/M. Worth paying for when multimodal capability is the actual requirement.

Chinese-language work: GLM-5 at $1.92/M or GLM-4-9B at $0.01/M for lighter tasks.

Reasoning-heavy, budget not an issue: Kimi K2.5 at $3.00/M. Bring it out for specific projects where the client's ROI justifies the spend.

Coding-heavy general work: Qwen3-Coder-30B at $0.35/M or DeepSeek Coder at $0.25/M.

All of these route through the same OpenAI-compatible client at https://global-apis.com/v1, which means you can A/B test models by literally changing one string. That alone saved me dozens of integration hours compared to wiring up four separate vendor SDKs.

Two code snippets that'll get you started:

python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(
api_key="ga_xxxxxxxxxxxx",
base_url="https://global-apis.com/v1"
)
response = client.chat.completions.create(
model="deepseek-v4-flash",
messages=[{
"role": "user",

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode
